coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots in fried breadcrumbs: In vitro toxicity to normal rat kidney cells and its control method
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Jie
Yuan, Shaofeng
Guo, Yahui
Yu, Hang
Cheng, Yuliang
Xie, Yunfei
Qian, He
Yao, Weirong
coredata.dc:description: In vitro toxicity of carbon dots (CDs) in unfried and fried breadcrumbs was evaluated using NRK cells to analyze the harm of excessive frying and the factors causing the increase of toxicity during processing and cooking. The results showed that the toxicity of CDs in market breadcrumbs increased by an astonishing 230 times after frying at 180 °C. Subsequent experiments showed that cell cycle was arrested at the G0/G1 phase when NRK cells were treated with CDs, which ultimately led to apoptosis and necrosis. The mechanism was identified as CDs-induced the production of ROS, resulting in the loss of mitochondrial membrane potential (67.00 ± 1.80%) and triggered early apoptosis. Furthermore, the release of inclusions from necrotic cell lysis led to the synthesis of upstream mediators HMGB-1 and TGF-β1, which increased downstream pro-inflammatory factors (TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6) and decreased anti-inflammatory factor (IL-10). The main measure to reduce toxicity was to control the amount of sucrose added. These results were useful in understanding the negative impact of excessively fried food and provided an idea for reducing the production of food-borne CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fried breadcrumbs
Normal rat kidney cells
Toxicity control

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The isolation and characterization of CDs
The effect of CDs on cell viability
The inhibition of cell growth by CDs
Factors affecting the toxicity of CDs
The effect of components on cytotoxicity in vitro
The effect of components on yield
The effect of frying oil type and used times
Declaration of Generative (AI) and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work described in this article was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (32372421 , 32302247 , 32172326) and Jiangxi Province High-Level Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team Project (jxsq2023105005).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of CCK-8 assay. Cell viability of NRK cells after 12 h treatment with various concentrations of (a) CDs-0, (b) CDs-120, (c) CDs-150, and (d) CDs-180. The logit and weibull model fitting results of (e) CDs-150 and (f) CDs-180. CDs-0 and CDs-180 represent the CDs extracted from native breadcrumbs and breadcrumbs after frying at 180 ℃, respectively.

The results of cell cycle. Flow cytometry analysis results of (a) the control, (b) IC10, (c) IC20, (d) IC30, (e) IC40, (f) IC50, and (g) CDs-150. (h) The mean ± SD of cell cycle distribution for each group was expressed based on three independent experiments.

The results of cell apoptosis. Representative flow cytometry analysis of (a) the control, (b) IC10, (c) IC20, (d) IC30, (e) IC40, (f) IC50, and (g) CDs-150. (h) The mean ± SD of four quadrant distribution of cell apoptosis for each group was expressed based on three independent experiments.

The determination of intracellular ROS. Representative flow cytometry analysis of (a) the control, (b) IC10, (c) IC20, (d) IC30, (e) IC40, (f) IC50, and (g) CDs-150. (h) The mean ± SD of fluorescence intensity for each group was expressed based on three independent experiments.

The determination of MMP. Representative flow cytometry analysis of (a) the control, (b) IC10, (c) IC20, (d) IC30, (e) IC40, (f) IC50, and (g) CDs-150. (h) The mean ± SD of loss of MMP for each group was expressed based on three independent experiments.
The determination of inflammatory cytokines. Changes in (a) HMGB-1, (b) TGF-β1, (c) TNF-α, (d) IL-1β, (e) IL-6, and (f) IL-10.

The effect of components type on cytotoxicity of CDs. Cell viability of NRK cells treated by CDs of (a) the control, (b) CaSO4, (c) SDG, (d) VitC, (e) Salt, (f) Butter, (g) Sucrose, and (h) Summary groups, respectively.
The effect of sucrose addition on cytotoxicity of CDs. Cell viability of NRK cells treated by CDs of (a) 0 g, (b) 5 g, (c) 10 g, (d) 15 g, (e) 20 g, and (f) 25 g groups, respectively.
The effect of components on yield of CDs. CDs yield of (a) different ingredient groups and (b) different sucrose addition groups.
The effect of frying oil on CDs. Effect of frying oil type and used times on (a) the yield of CDs and (b) cytotoxicity of CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots in fried breadcrumbs: In vitro toxicity to normal rat kidney cells and its control method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control, Jiangnan University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control, Jiangnan University, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Jiangnan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In vitro toxicity of carbon dots (CDs) in unfried and fried breadcrumbs was evaluated using NRK cells to analyze the harm of excessive frying and the factors causing the increase of toxicity during processing and cooking. The results showed that the toxicity of CDs in market breadcrumbs increased by an astonishing 230 times after frying at 180 °C. Subsequent experiments showed that cell cycle was arrested at the G0/G1 phase when NRK cells were treated with CDs, which ultimately led to apoptosis and necrosis. The mechanism was identified as CDs-induced the production of ROS, resulting in the loss of mitochondrial membrane potential (67.00 ± 1.80%) and triggered early apoptosis. Furthermore, the release of inclusions from necrotic cell lysis led to the synthesis of upstream mediators HMGB-1 and TGF-β1, which increased downstream pro-inflammatory factors (TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6) and decreased anti-inflammatory factor (IL-10). The main measure to reduce toxicity was to control the amount of sucrose added. These results were useful in understanding the negative impact of excessively fried food and provided an idea for reducing the production of food-borne CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The impact of nanoparticles on different organs was contingent upon their distinct properties. The intake of gold nanoparticles, for example, may lead to the onset of hepatitis [1]. Gold nanoparticles’ size influenced the toxicity on various cell types, with larger particles typically having a greater potential for toxicity and the potential to transition from apoptosis to necrosis [2]. The toxicity of graphene nanoparticles is highly dependent on their shape [3]. C60 NPs with strong redox properties may affect the brain because of unreversed oxidative damage [4]. Meanwhile, Carbon dots (CDs) have been found to only produce toxic effects on human lung cancer cell lines (H596，H446 and Calu-1) at 0.002 μg/mL. In fact, the half inhibitory concentration (IC50) for CDs is significantly lower than that of other nanoparticles, at just 0.02 μg/mL [5]. Given their smaller particle size and greater ease of absorption by the body, much attention has been focused on the potential toxicity of CDs to human health. Of particular concern is the potential for food-borne CDs to be produced within food due to high temperatures during processing and cooking, which could then penetrate organisms in significant quantities through food consumption. Additionally, compared to traditional CDs, they may have a more profound impact on organisms [6]. The presence of CDs in breadcrumbs was confirmed, and the effect of frying on their properties was further explored by our previous work [7]. Through in vitro digestion and binding experiments with human serum albumin (HSA), it was discovered that these CDs were very stable and could bind with HSA, facilitating their distribution throughout different body parts. A research showed that CDs had the potential to accumulate in the kidney and cause harm [6]. To further investigate this issue, this study wanted to use normal rat kidney (NRK) cells to assess the toxicity of CDs present in breadcrumbs.

Nanoparticles' cytotoxicity mechanism is predominantly analyzed by examining changes in DNA, oxidative stress, and inflammatory cytokines. CDs in roasted salmon can permeate to nucleus and stimulate most cells to initiate the cell cycle at high concentrations, thus disrupting the cellular process [6]. CDs in bread can significantly impact the balance of intracellular redox homeostasis because they affect some important genes that can damage mitochondrial membranes and produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), like p53, CYP1A, Bcl-2, CAT, and SOD [8,9]. These ROS cased acute oxidative stress, which could ultimately lead to apoptosis [10]. Moreover, the production of ROS and necrosis of cells can also activate pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as interleukin-1β (IL-1β) and tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), and induce inflammation [11]. Therefore, the main emphasis in examining the cytotoxicity of CDs in breadcrumbs is on their capacity to induce oxidative stress and stimulate proinflammatory cytokines.

Breadcrumbs can be made in two ways: fermented or puffed. Fermented breadcrumbs are made traditionally by baking bread, which is then crushed and dried. Puffed breadcrumbs are made using extrusion and drying, and do not require yeast fermentation. Fermented breadcrumbs have a better color and aroma, and can withstand frying without falling apart. Puffed breadcrumbs, on the other hand, are quick to produce but have limited applications because they dissolve in water and are not suitable for frozen food products. This study focused on fermented breadcrumbs, which have a wider range of applications. Fermented breadcrumbs are made by seasoning wheat flour, water, and yeast with sucrose, vegetable oil, sodium chloride, and functional food additives such as calcium sulfate, vitamin C, and single double glycer (SDG).

The materials for preparing CDs such as breadcrumbs were the same as the previous study [7]. Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) (Adamas life C8010–500 mL)、Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) (Adamas life C8013–500 mL)、Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) (Adamas life C8010–500 mL)、Penicillin-Streptomycin (Adamas life C8021–100 mL) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Trypsin Solution (0.25%) without EDTA (C0205)，Enhanced Cell Counting Kit-8 (C0042), Cell Cycle and Apoptosis Analysis Kit (C1052), Annexin V-FITC Apoptosis Detection Kit (C1062M), ROS Assay Kit (S0033S), and Enhanced mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit with JC-1 (C2003S) were provided by Beyotime Biotech Inc. (Shanghai, China). Rat High Mobility Group Protein B1 (HMGB-1) ELISA Kit (E-EL-R0505c) was purchased from Elabscience Biotechnology Co.,Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Rat tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α) ELISA Kit (EK0526), Rat interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β) ELISA Kit (EK0393), Rat IL-6 ELISA Kit (EK0412), Rat IL-10 ELISA Kit (EK0418), and Rat transforming growth factor beta 1 (TGF-β1) ELISA Kit (EK0514) were obtained from Boster Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The isolation and characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The breadcrumbs were fried at 120 °C, 150 °C, and 180 °C for 5 min, respectively. Then CDs were extracted and characterized according to the previous study [7]. After frying, the oil was first removed by petroleum ether, and the primary extract was obtained by stirring 50% ethanol overnight and ultrasonic extraction. The primary extract was concentrated and evaporated to dryness, redissolved with the ethanol-methanol mixture (v:v = 9:1), condensed and evaporated again, and redissolved with water. Then, the solution was dialyzed with a 3.5 kDa dialysis bag and purified by Waters auto purification HPLC instrument (2545, Waters, Shanghai, China) to obtain CDs. Then, a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100 F, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), a Nicolet-iS10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, America), Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, America), and an ultrafleXtreme MALDI-TOF/TOF-MS (Bruker, Billerica, America) were used to analyze the structure of CDs.

Researches revealed that CDs mainly caused damage to kidney organs [12,13], so NRK cells were selected as the cell model for this study. NRK cells were obtained from National Collection of Authenticated Cell Cultures (Shanghai, China). The cells were cultured in a growth medium [10% (v/v) FBS, 1% (v/v) Penicillin-Streptomycin, 89% (v/v) DMEM] in T25 cm2 flasks and 6-well or 96-well culture plates (Corning) at 37 ℃ in 5% CO2. CDs were dissolved in DMEM for in vitro study. The cells of 13 generations and below were used to perform all experiments.

The assay was based on previous study [14]. NRK cells were seeded in 96-well culture plates containing 100 μL growth medium and 5000 cells per well. After 24 h of culture, the growth medium was discarded and washed third with DMEM. The cells were exposed to CDs for 12 h after 200 μL of DMEM containing different concentrations of CDs (from 0 to 100 mg/mL) were added to per well. DMEM containing CDs was discarded and washed third with DMEM. The CCK-8 and DMEM mixture (v:v = 10:1) was added and incubated for 1 h. A microplate reader (Epoch2, BioTek, Winooski, Vermont, USA) was used at both 450 nm for measurement and 650 nm for reference. The percentage of cell viability was calculated using the formula (1):
(1)
CV
=
(A 2 -A 0 )/(A 1 -A 0 )×100%
where CV is the cell viability (%), A1 is the absorbance of the wells with cells, CCK-8 but without CDs, A2 is the absorbance of the wells with cells, CCK-8 and CDs, A0 is the absorbance of the wells with CCK-8 but without cells.

The appropriate concentration was selected and fitted using formulas (2) and (3). IC10, IC20, IC30, IC40, and IC50 were calculated based on the best fitting results and used in subsequent cell research [15].
(2)
1-CV
=
1/(1+exp(-α-βlog
10
(c)))
(3)
1-CV
=
1-exp(-exp(α+βlog
10
(c)))
where α and β are model parameters, c is the concentration of CDs (mM).

The experiment referred to existing researches [8,9]. The growth of NRK cells were initiated by seeding 1 × 105 cells in 6-well culture plates, each containing 2 mL of growth medium. NRK cells were seeded in 6-well culture plates, with 1 × 105 cells and 2 mL growth medium per well. After 12 h of culture, the cells were treated with CDs (dissolved in the growth medium) and cultured for 12 h. Based on the instructions from the manufacturer, both treated and untreated cells were gathered and fixed in 70% cold ethanol at 4 °C for 16 h. Following this, the fixed cells underwent centrifugation at 1000g for 5 min, and were washed twice with PBS. 0.5 mL of propidium staining solution was added to each tube containing cell samples, and the cells were resuspended and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Finally, the cells were detected using flow cytometry (FACSAriaII, Buddy Corporation, USA).

The experiment was based on previous studies [6,8,9]. The growth of NRK cells was consistent with Section 2.5. The specific culture conditions and CDs intervention conditions were the same as Section 2.4. According to manufacturer’s instructions, treated and untreated cells were collected. Collected cells were stained with Annexin V-FITC and PI. Then cells were incubated at 25 °C for 20 min. Finally, fluorescence intensity of cells was detected using a flow cytometry (FACSAriaII, Buddy, New Jersey, America).

Intracellular ROS was analyzed by using DCFH-DA [12]. The experiment before probe being loaded to the cells was consistent with Section 2.6. After removing the growth medium, the cells in each well were treated with DCFH-DA dissolved in DMEM (1 mL, 10 μM), and incubated under optimal conditions (37 °C, 20 min). The cells were then gently washed three times with DMEM, trypsinized and gathered. The ROS of cells was determined by a flow cytometry.

JC-1 fluorescent probe was used to detect mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) in this experiment, and MMP was analyzed by the change of fluorescence intensity ratio of 530 nm (green) under 490 nm excitation and 590 nm (red) under 525 nm excitation [12,14]. The experiment before loading the probe to the cells was consistent with Section 2.6. The NRK cells were stained with JC-1, and incubated. After being washed twice using JC-1 staining buffer, the stained cells were examined through a high-resolution confocal laser microscope (LSM880, Carl Zeiss, Germany). For flow cytometer analysis, the cells were trypsinized, resuspended in JC-1 staining buffer and analyzed.

The changes in inflammatory cytokines can be determined by enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). The cell culture and treatment were consistent with Section 2.6. After collecting the cell culture supernatants, they were subjected to centrifugation at 4 °C and 1000g for 20 min. The resulting supernatant was used for ELISA assays. All assays were performed according to the manufacturer’s instructions, and the content of inflammatory cytokines were calculated according to their respective standard curves.

The ingredients mainly refer to the corresponding national standards and literature [16]. The ingredients were added to the bread machine according to the formula instructions, stirred and woken for 20 min firstly, and then fermented at about 30 °C for 60 min, and then taken out and placed in the oven (preheated to 180 °C in advance) for baking for 20 min. After baking, it was taken out and cooled, then sliced, crushed and placed in an oven (preheated to 110 °C in advance) to dry to make breadcrumbs.

The experiments were conducted in triplicate and the outcomes shown were indicative of three separate experiments. Data analysis was performed using SPSS-26 (IBM, NY, America), with the results being presented as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance was considered at P < 0.05. Flow jo (BD, New Jersey, US) and Modfit (VSH, Bedford, MA) were used to analyze the cell results. Origin 2023 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA) was used to create all the figures.

In a previous study, CDs-0 and CDs-180 were thoroughly examined and employed to investigate how frying affected the CDs presented in breadcrumbs [7]. In this study, to further verify the effect of frying on inner CDs of breadcrumbs, CDs-120 and CDs-150 were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The results were shown in Fig. S1a-d. The average particle sizes of CDs-120 and CDs-150 were 3.24 ± 0.61 nm and 2.98 ± 0.56 nm, respectively. The changes further validated the previous research that with the increase in temperature, particle size distribution would be more uniform, and the particle size would be gradually reduced. HRTEM images also showed that CDs-120 and CDs-150 had the same lattice spacing (both 0.20 nm) as CDs-0 and CDs-180. The properties of fluorescence and UV–vis absorption of CDs were shown in Fig. S1e and f. Compared with previous research [7], they had the same fluorescence properties and the UV–vis absorption peak of CDs red shifted with frying temperature increased. Fig. S2a and b showed the FT-IR and XPS spectra of CDs-120 and CDs-150. Compared with CDs-120, the peaks of CDs-150′s FT-IR spectra had different degrees of blue shift. N content increased from 1.57% to 1.91%, and the carbon to oxygen atomic ratio (RC/O) changed from 1.22 to 1.33. The changes indicated that higher temperature was beneficial to aggravate the carbonization of CDs, consistent with the previous research results [7]. The results ultimately proved the existence of CDs in fried breadcrumbs, which were influenced by frying temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CDs on cell viability
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effects of frying temperature on CDs toxicity in breadcrumbs, CDs were extracted from breadcrumbs that had been fried at four different temperatures, including 0 °C (not fried), 120 °C, 150 °C, and 180 °C were studied. These CDs were labelled CDs-0, CDs-120, CDs-150, and CDs-180, respectively. The results presented in Fig. 1a-d demonstrated that four different types of CDs had a concentration-dependent effect on the activity of NRK cells. At high concentrations, cell viability was lost within 12 h. Based on mass concentration, CDs-120, CDs-150, and CDs-180 exhibited approximately 5, 145, and 230 times more toxicity than CDs-0, respectively. This indicated that the toxicity of CDs found in breadcrumbs was influenced by the frying temperature, with higher temperatures resulting in greater toxicity. This conclusion aligned with the finding that the toxicity of CDs in roast pork increased with rising temperatures [13]. The toxicity of CDs-120 was similar to that of CDs-0. However, the toxicity of CDs escalated notably when the frying temperature hit 150 °C. Furthermore, as the frying temperature exceeded 180 °C, the toxicity of CDs rose further, indicating that high frying temperatures caused a substantial increase in toxicity. To eliminate variations in breadcrumbs, several other breadcrumbs in marketing were selected for validation. After frying and extracting CDs, NRK cells were subjected to a treatment of 0.3 mg/mL CDs, the results of which were presented in Fig. S3. The results showed that CDs presented in fried breadcrumbs exhibited significantly cytotoxicity to NRK cells after high-temperature frying. And this phenomenon is not an exception and highlights the potential health risks associated with the consumption of such food products. Therefore, CDs-180 was chosen for the main research object in this experiment, and the CDs-150 group was set as the positive control group. Studies have shown that the molecular weight of CDs could be represented by the highest peak in the MALDI-TOF-MS spectra. The results of CDs-150 were shown in Fig. S2c, and the MALDI-TOF-MS spectra of CDs-180 have been measured in the previous study [7]. The results of the Logit and Weibull models which can conduct more accurate toxicity experiments on CDs-150 and CDs-180 were shown in Fig. 1e and f and Table S1 [15]. Upon comparison, it was found that the Weibull model demonstrated better consistency in inhibiting NRK cells by the two CDs. Additionally, the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) calculated by the Weibull model revealed that the toxicity of CDs-180 was approximately 1.5 times that of CDs-150. These results further emphasized that frying breadcrumbs at high temperatures could make higher toxic level of CDs. For the following cell experiments, NRK cells were exposed to different amounts of CDs-180, namely IC10 (0.317 mM), IC20 (0.331 mM), IC30 (0.341 mM), IC40 (0.348 mM), and IC50 (0.355 mM). The NRK cells were treated with 0.355 mM CDs-150 as the positive control group, while NRK cells not treated with CDs served as the control group. Through this experimental design, the hazards of CDs in breadcrumbs and excessive frying were compared and analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The inhibition of cell growth by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: More details about cell growth inhibition can be gained by conducting cell cycle and apoptosis experiments [17]. As illustrated in Fig. 2, the impact of CDs on the cell cycle of NRK cells was significant (P < 0.05). The number of NRK cells in the S and G2/M phases increased significantly (P < 0.05), from 7.977 ± 0.824% to 11.063 ± 2.043% and 13.770 ± 1.725%, respectively, upon treatment with IC10 and IC20. This increase could be attributed to the lower concentrations of CDs-180 inhibiting the division of NRK cells [18]. In the following experimental group, the S phase almost disappeared (28.380 ± 0.509% to 7.703 ± 1.145%) with the increasing concentration of CDs-180. The G2 phase gradually decreased, indicating possible DNA damage in NRK cells, leading to G1 phase arrest [19]. However, the S phase of NRK cells treated with CDs-150 significantly increased from 28.380 ± 0.509% to 35.513 ± 2.162%, indicating that most cells remained active. According to the results of the apoptosis experiments ( Fig. 3), there was a link between the amount of CDs-180 and the cell count in quadrants Q1 and Q3. A significant increase in the proportion of Q1 from 2.573 ± 0.730% to 28.217 ± 2.784%, and Q3 from 0.58 ± 0.342% to 20.560 ± 0.711% was found, indicating that high concentrations of CDs-180 can lead to necrocytosis and early apoptosis [6,17]. Notably, the NRK cells treated with CDs-150 displayed a rise in the amount of cells in the Q2 quadrant from 7.130 ± 0.426% to 10.613 ± 1.786%, with no significant increase in the number of cells in Q1 and Q3 quadrants, meaning the cells remained healthy. The result implied that the toxicity of CDs in breadcrumbs significantly increased after high-temperature frying (P < 0.05).

Recent researches revealed that elevated ROS level can result in damage to cell DNA, leading to cell necrosis and apoptosis [4,6,20]. Therefore, the intracellular ROS content was measured to explore whether CDs-180 can promote ROS production in NRK cells. The outcomes, displayed in Fig. 4, revealed that as the concentration of CDs-180 increased, the fluorescence peak of DCF (generated by the reaction of DCFH and ROS) significantly increased (P < 0.05). The fluorescence intensity in the IC50 group was approximately twice as high as that of the control group, while the NRK cells of CDs-150 group was at a comparable level with the control. This suggested that CDs-180 can affect the activity of cells by promoting the production of ROS, and higher frying temperatures can make the effect of CDs in breadcrumbs on ROS stronger.

The decrease in MMP which is a hallmark of early apoptosis can be caused by increased ROS level [20]. Therefore, JC-1 dye was used to detect MMP to further verify CDs-180 can induce early apoptosis of NRK cells. Then, the relative proportion of red and green fluorescence intensity, which was affected by the MMP's role in accumulating JC-1 in mitochondrial matrix, was calculated. Fig. 5 showed the results obtained by flow cytometry analysis, where the Q2 region represented red fluorescence intensity, and the Q3 region represented green fluorescence intensity. As the concentration of CDs-180 increased, the Q3 region increased, meaning the MMP decreased, the same as the effect of CDs on MMP in roast beef [12]. The addition of CDs-180 caused the Q3 region to rose from 16.87 ± 1.44% to 67.00 ± 1.80%. According to the results in Fig. S4, which were captured using a confocal laser microscope, each group had different cell behaviors. The red fluorescence intensity decreased and the green fluorescence intensity increased with the increasing of CDs-180′s concentration. This confirmed the result obtained by the flow cytometer analysis (Fig. 5). Fig. S4 also showed that CDs-180 not only caused a decrease in MMP but also altered the shape of cells, making them round and decreasing the contact between cells. These changes were similar to what happened during apoptosis, confirming CDs-180-indeed apoptosis [18]. These results were consistent with the previous study [14]. On the other hand, the positive group showed that CDs-150 had smaller effect on MMP when used at high concentrations. The proportion of the Q3 region was 27.13 ± 2.15%, which was similar to the IC10 group. What is more, the cells under a microscope remained healthy and polygonal in shape. This also directly proved that low-temperature frying was beneficial for reducing CDs' toxicity in breadcrumbs compared with high-temperature frying.

The intracellular material will be released into the environment when cell necrosis occurs, which triggers inflammation of cells [21]. Additionally, a rise in ROS levels can further exacerbate this reaction [22,23]. To better study the impact of CDs-180 on cellular damage and confirm the occurrence of necrosis, the effect of CDs-180 exposure on the levels of inflammatory cytokines were analyzed. The results were displayed in Fig. 6. HMGB-1 is a nuclear DNA binding protein and helps maintain the stability of nucleosomes and gene transcription. It serves as an important medium of inflammation when it is released from necrotic cells [24]. HMGB-1 also acts as a significant upstream mediator of inflammatory cytokines, and its activation indicates an intracellular inflammatory response [24]. Results from Fig. 6a demonstrated that as the concentration of CDs-180 increased, the content of HMGB-1 also increased in a dose-dependent manner (P < 0.05). In the IC50 group, the amount of HMGB-1 rose from 2.307 ± 0.156 ng/mL to 14.007 ± 0.162 ng/mL, which was approximately six times higher than that of the control group. This finding suggested that high doses of CDs-180 may increase the content of upstream mediators of inflammatory cytokines. The presence of TGF-β1 is closely linked to the development of nephropathy, such as mesangial proliferative nephritis [25]. Therefore, change in TGF-β1 levels is particularly important in understanding the effect of CDs in breadcrumbs on the kidney. In Fig. 6b, it can be observed that as the concentration of CDs-180 increased, so did the concentration of TGF-β1 (to levels of 66.673 ± 2.894, 147.930 ± 2.732, and 198.860 ± 3.630 pg/mL, respectively). These levels were 1.158, 2.569, and 3.454 times higher than the control group, suggesting that CDs-180 may cause cell damage and initiate an inflammatory reaction. The results of detecting downstream pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 (Fig. 6c-e) were consistent with the findings of HMGB-1 and TGF-β1, indicating a dose-dependent increase (P < 0.05). At the highest dose, CDs-180 increased the pro-inflammatory cytokines to 7.218, 4.939, and 4.337 times of the control group, further highlighting the occurrence of cellular inflammatory response with the addition of CDs-180 [26,27]. Moreover, the content of anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 (Fig. 6f) increased at low doses, but decreased at high doses of CDs-180. This could be due to the fact that low-dose CDs-180 can lead to cell inflammation, but cells can regulate inflammation by producing anti-inflammatory cytokines [28]. However, high doses of CDs-180 induced DNA damage, triggered the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines while impeding the release of anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the toxicity of CDs
The effect of components on cytotoxicity in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of various components in breadcrumbs on the toxicity of CDs was investigated. The control group consisted of CDs extracted from fried breadcrumbs made from 200 g of wheat flour, 100 g of water, and 2 g of dry yeast powder [16]. Fig. 7 depicted that, with the exception of sucrose, the other additives at the maximum allowable addition did not significantly affect the toxicity of CDs (P > 0.05). The sucrose group demonstrated a toxicity increase of more than 20 times compared to the control group, while the toxicity of CDs in the summary group (containing all additives) was elevated by about 10 times. These findings suggested that the addition of significant amounts of sucrose could lead to a surge in toxicity, indicating the need to regulate the amount of sucrose added to mitigate the toxicity of CDs. The experimental results in Fig. 8 demonstrated a positive correlation between the amount of sucrose added and the toxicity of CDs to NRK cells. The toxicity was approximately 8 times higher than the blank group when 20 g/200 g of wheat flour was added. Therefore, it was recommended that the addition of sucrose should be limited to 20 g/200 g of wheat flour from a cytotoxicity perspective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of components on yield
ce:section.ce:para: A comprehensive analysis of the control of harmful substances necessitates an examination from two perspectives: absolute toxicity and effective toxicity [30]. Absolute toxicity pertains to the measurement of the dose at which the toxic substance itself causes harm to the organism, as evidenced by the data presented in Section 3.7.1. Effective toxicity, on the other hand, refers to actual toxicity, which is based on absolute toxicity, and takes into account the volatility and content of harmful substances. Therefore, to reduce the toxicity of CDs in fried breadcrumbs, it is imperative to investigate ways of minimizing its yield. Fig. 9a illustrated that sucrose's addition crucially influenced the CDs yield. The summary group's CDs yield was similar to the sucrose group, further validating the influence of sucrose on CDs yield. Excessive sucrose addition caused a surplus of the Maillard reaction, which provided an ideal condition for CDs production, as previously established by various studies [13,31]. Consequently, the impact of sucrose addition on CDs yield was examined further, and the findings were depicted in Fig. 9b. The study revealed that there was no notable difference in CDs content in breadcrumbs with 200 g of wheat flour mass and an additional amount of sucrose of less than 10 g (P > 0.05). However, when the supplementary sucrose quantity reached 15 g/200 g of wheat flour, CDs content increased by 2.16 times, with a significant difference (P < 0.05). Therefore, sucrose addition should be controlled within 10 g/200 g of wheat flour to maintain optimum CDs content. Other additives had little effect on the toxicity of CDs, and only need to meet the national standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of frying oil type and used times
ce:section.ce:para: It is important to note that the frying process can affect the toxicity of CDs, in addition to the influence of ingredients. Apart from the frying temperature and time, the edible oil used also plays a crucial role. To decrease the toxicity of CDs further, the effects of edible oil types and used times on the toxicity of CDs were studied. The results were shown in Fig. 10. The findings indicated that edible oil did not significantly impact the yield and IC50 of CDs. However, a discernible effect on CDs was observed when oil was repeatedly used beyond 5 times. This may be attributed to the generation of oxidation substances [32], which are known to impair the properties of CDs [33]. Consequently, it was recommended that the usage of edible oil for frying is limited to a maximum of five times to preserve the attributes of CDs.

When frying breadcrumbs, the level of toxicity in CDs was greatly affected by the temperature. The higher the frying temperature, the higher the toxicity, up to 230 times higher at 180 °C. CDs-180 was found to cause DNA damage and cell death at low concentrations, and both necrosis and apoptosis at high concentrations. Exposure to CDs-180 also increased the production of ROS, which led to changes in cell morphology and a loss of MMP. The levels of inflammatory cytokines and mediators were also found to be affected by high-dose CDs-180 exposure.

Toxicity control can be effectively carried out in three ways. Firstly, it was important to control the amount of sucrose added during the production of breadcrumbs. It should be kept within 10 g/200 g of wheat flour. Secondly, it was recommended to reduce frying temperature and time as much as possible. Lastly, it was best to avoid reusing edible oil more than 5 times to prevent any adverse effects.

Yao Weirong: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Qian He: Supervision, Project administration. Yu Hang: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Guo Yahui: Investigation. Xie Yunfei: Validation, Resources. Cheng Yuliang: Validation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yuan Shaofeng: Writing – review & editing, Software. Yin Jie: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative (AI) and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the authors used grammarly in order to improve readability and language. After using this service, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that would influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this article was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (32372421 , 32302247 , 32172326) and Jiangxi Province High-Level Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team Project (jxsq2023105005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.procbio.2024.01.026.

ce:section.ce:para:
